planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; aquatic milkweed grows naturally in swamp forests, streambanks, and floodplain margins across the Gulf Coastal Plain. In zone 9, it may remain semi-evergreen in mild winters.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; aquatic milkweed is the only native milkweed in North America adapted to the forested swamp conditions of the Gulf Coastal Plain — growing naturally in the shade of bottomland forests, swamp edges, and along bayous and sluggish streams from Florida to Texas and north to Illinois. Unlike most milkweeds that demand dry, sunny conditions, this species thrives in wet, partially shaded sites where it provides monarch butterfly larval habitat in habitats otherwise lacking milkweed. The tiny white flowers in rounded clusters are delicate and fragrant. As a monarch host plant, it is critically important because it blooms in two seasons — spring and fall — aligning with monarch migration and breeding in the Gulf South. An outstanding zone 9a milkweed for shaded, wet areas and streamside gardens where no other milkweed will thrive.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for cold stratification and spring germination; or cold stratify seeds for 30 days and sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost. White milkweed grows naturally in dry to well-drained upland woods and woodland edges, tolerating more shade than most milkweeds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; white milkweed is one of the few native milkweeds adapted to grow in the dappled shade of open woodlands and forest edges rather than in open sun — a significant advantage for gardeners who want to support monarchs and other milkweed-dependent species in partially shaded situations. The rounded clusters of pure white flowers with a distinctive dark purple crown in the center are among the most attractive of any milkweed, combining visual elegance with remarkable fragrance. Growing naturally in dry to well-drained upland forest understories across the southeastern US, it is more drought-tolerant than its woodland habitat would suggest. An excellent companion for native ferns, wild ginger, and other woodland plants where it provides the critical role of milkweed host in shaded garden zones.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in wet to moist, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; longleaf milkweed is a specialist of wet pine savannas and flatwoods, requiring wet, acidic, nutrient-poor, sandy soil that mimics its natural firebreak-maintained longleaf pine habitat. Avoid rich or amended soils.

directions: Plant in moist to seasonally wet, sandy, acidic, nutrient-poor soil in full sun; longleaf milkweed is a native of the wet pine savannas, flatwoods, and pocosins of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, growing in the unique combination of wet, acidic sand and frequent fire disturbance that characterizes longleaf pine ecosystems — one of the most biodiverse and most threatened habitats in North America. It has distinctively long, narrow, grass-like leaves (giving the species its common name) that are quite unlike the broader leaves of most milkweeds, making it texturally interesting in the garden. The pink-tinged white flowers attract monarchs and specialist milkweed bees. Because it requires wet, acidic, lean sand, it performs best in zone 9 coastal gardens with naturally sandy, poorly drained soils and is an exceptional plant for wet, acidic areas in the garden that challenge most other species.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, lean, sandy or rocky, acidic to neutral soil; many-flowered beardtongue is a Florida endemic that grows naturally in dry, open longleaf pine flatwoods and sandy scrub. It blooms in its first year from fall-sown seed.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; many-flowered beardtongue is a Florida and coastal Georgia endemic, notable for producing an exceptionally large number of white tubular flowers densely packed on tall, erect flower stems in late spring — the most floriferous of the native southeastern penstemons. Like all penstemons, it requires excellent drainage and lean soil; rich, amended soils cause rapid decline. The white flowers are pollinated by bumblebees and native solitary bees. This species is well-adapted to the Gulf Coast region and thrives in zone 9 sandy gardens where most penstemons struggle with heat and humidity. An excellent white-flowered native for dry, sunny borders in zone 8–10 where it blooms prolifically in late spring before heat sets in. Short-lived perennial that maintains itself by self-seeding in appropriate sandy soil.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in wet to moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; allegheny monkey flower grows in standing shallow water and saturated soils along stream banks, pond margins, and wet meadows. Seeds are tiny — surface sow without covering.

directions: Surface sow on moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil at pond margins, stream banks, and rain garden edges in partial shade to full sun; allegheny monkey flower is a native perennial of wet streambanks and pond edges across most of eastern North America, producing bright lavender-blue tubular flowers with distinctive two-lobed lips that give the plant a fanciful face resembling a grinning monkey — the whimsical origin of the common name shared across the Mimulus genus. The square stems are typical of the mint family to which it belongs. It grows in standing water up to 2 inches deep and in perpetually saturated soils at pond edges, making it excellent for naturalistic water garden margins. An important nectar plant for bumblebees and hummingbirds. Blooms from early summer into fall with consistent moisture. Self-sows freely along muddy stream banks. Excellent for naturalizing wet sites where its spreading colonies create a vivid blue summer display.
